paper_title: Highly visible light responsive metal loaded N\/TiO2 nanoparticles for photocatalytic conversion of CO2 into methane

catalyst.source: This study demonstrates that Ag-NTiO 2 is a promising photocatalytic material for photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 into hydrocarbons under visible light irradiation.
catalyst.context: Photocatalytic reduction of carbon dioxide (CO 2 ) into valuable hydrocarbon such as methane (CH 4 ) using water as reducing agent is a good strategy for environment and energy applications.In this study, a facile and simple sol-gel method was employed for the synthesis of metal (Cu and Ag) loaded nanosized NTiO 2 photocatalyst.The prepared photocatalysts were characterized by X-ray diffraction, transmission electron microscopy, BET Surface area analyzer, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and UV-vis diffuses reflectance spectroscopy.The photocatalytic conversion of CO 2 into methane was carried out under visible light irradiation (420 nm) by prepared photocatalysts in order to evaluate the photocatalytic efficiency.The results demonstrate that Ag loaded NTiO 2 showed enhanced photocatalytic performance for methane production from CO 2 compared to other Cu-NTiO 2 , NTiO 2 and TiO 2 photocatalysts.The improvement in the photocatalytic activity could be attributed to high specific surface area, extended visible light absorption and suppressed recombination of electronhole pair due to synergistic effects of silver and nitrogen in the Ag-NTiO 2 photocatalyst.This study demonstrates that Ag-NTiO 2 is a promising photocatalytic material for photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 into hydrocarbons under visible light irradiation.

catalyst.llm generation: nitrogen doped TiO2, nitrogen co-doped TiO2
catalyst.source: In the typical process, the nitrogen doped TiO 2 and metal ions (MCu and Ag) and nitrogen co-doped TiO 2 were prepared using solgel method according to our previous study with little modification 29.
catalyst.context: In the typical process, the nitrogen doped TiO 2 and metal ions (MCu and Ag) and nitrogen co-doped TiO 2 were prepared using solgel method according to our previous study with little modification 29.Firstly, required amount of tetra-butyl titanate was slowly dissolved in a mix solution of ethanol and acetic acid (4010 ml) and stirred for half an hour at room temperature (Solution A).Secondly, the required amount of urea, Cu(NO 3 ) 2 3H 2 O or Ag NO 3 were dissolved in 25 ml mix solution of ethanol and acetic acid (205 ml) under vigorous stirring at room temperature for 20 min (solution B).Then in the next step, solution B was added drop wise to solution A with rate 2 ml per minute under vigorous stirring within 12.5 min.For homogeneous solution, the mixture was stirrer for another 3 h at 50 C.After this, obtained mixture was kept at room temperature in air for 24 h to form homogeneous gel.The prepared TiO 2 based gels were dried at 80 C in microwave oven for 10 h.The finally obtained powders were calcined at 450 C for 3 h in the muffle furnace.The molar ratio of TiO 2 to urea was controlled as 1:2 in all nitrogen doped and co-doped samples, while Cu and Ag ratio to TiO 2 were used as1 atm. and 0.09 wt respectively which were previously optimized in our reports 30,31. The crystalline structural properties were studied using X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Dmax-3B) with Cu K radiation (V40 kV, I36 mA).The transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-1200EX electron microscope) operated at 200 kV was used for particle size and shape.The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET, ASAP 2020) surface areas (SBET) of the samples were calculated by nitrogen physisorption on a nitrogen adsorption apparatus at 77 K (Micromeritics Instruments).For the determination of electronic states and composition of prepared samples, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo-VG Scientific, ESCALAB250, monochromatic Al K Xray source) was used and binding energies were referenced to the C 1s peak of adventitious carbon at 284.8 eV.The UVVis diffuse reflectance spectra were measured on HITACHI U-4100 UV-vis spectrometer using BaSO 4 as a reference standard.The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were obtained using a HITACHI F-4500 Fluorescence spectrophotometer at excitation wavelength of 380 nm at room temperature. The photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 was performed in a gas closed circulation system under visible light irradiation at controlled temperature of 288 K with pressure of around 1.25 atm.A 300 W xenon lamp (Perfect Light PLSSXE300) with an optical filter (420 nm) was used as a light source.Certain amount of catalyst (50 mg) dispersed in deionized water (100 ml) by ultrasonic treatment in the reaction system.After evacuating the photo reactor, compressed CO 2 was purged into the system from inlet tube in the cylindrical quartz reactor.The resultant gas samples were analyzed online using a gas chromatograph (Agilent 7890A).

catalyst.source: Especially, the Ag-NTiO 2 photocatalyst has showed superior photocatalytic performance compared to other samples.
catalyst.context: Metal ion (Cu, Ag) loaded and nitrogen doped TiO 2 nanoparticles were successfully prepared by simple sol-gel method.The prepared nanoparticles showed increased light absorption in whole visible light region.Moreover, the results demonstrated excellent performance for photocatalytic production of methane from CO 2 under visible light irradiation.Especially, the Ag-NTiO 2 photocatalyst has showed superior photocatalytic performance compared to other samples.The enhanced efficiency of the photocatalysts can be attributed to high specific surface area, superior light absorption property, suppressed recombination of electronhole pairs during photocatalysis due to cooperative effects of metal ion and nitrogen doping in TiO 2 nanostructure.This work may be useful for the development of new novel TiO 2 based materials for the photoreduction of CO 2 into hydrocarbons for environment and energy applications. Fig. 1.XRD patterns of different samples prepared by sol-gel method and calcined at 450 C. mine the crystal phase composition of TiO 2 , NTiO 2 , Ag-NTiO 2 and Cu-NTiO 2 , as shown in Fig. 1.The XRD patterns show obvious peaks of (101), (004), (200), (211), (105), (204), (220) and (215) crystal planes at 2 angle of 25.36, 37.80, 48.19, 54.5, 55.2, 62.90, 69.91, 75.0 respectively, which all belong to anatase phase structure of TiO 2 (JCPDS-21-1272 Fig. 2. TEM images of (A) pure TiO 2 , (B) NTiO 2 , (C) Cu-NTiO 2 and (D) Cu-NTiO 2 nanoparticles. Fig. 3. (A) N 2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and (B) the corresponding BJH pore size distribution curves of different samples. Fig. 5. (A) UV-visible absorption spectra, (B) Tauc plot obtained from UV-vis absorption spectra of different prepared samples. Fig. 6. (A) Photocatalytic methane production from CO 2 in the presence of different catalysts under visible light irradiation (420 nm).(B) Production rate of methane from CO 2 for 1 h reaction by different photocatalysts. Fig. 7. Repeated reactions of CO 2 photocatalytic reduction over Ag-NTiO 2 under visible light irradiation (420 nm). Physical properties of metal loaded NTiO 2 nanoparticles. Acknowledgements N.R. Khalid thanks to Higher Education Commission of Pakistan for providing financial support under the project No: 21-1178SRGP R DHEC2016. Fig. 4. XPS core level spectra of Ag-NTiO 2 sample.

co-catalyst.source: while Cu and Ag ratio to TiO 2 were used as1 atm. and 0.09 wt respectively which were previously optimized in our reports 30,31.
co-catalyst.context: In the typical process, the nitrogen doped TiO 2 and metal ions (MCu and Ag) and nitrogen co-doped TiO 2 were prepared using solgel method according to our previous study with little modification 29.Firstly, required amount of tetra-butyl titanate was slowly dissolved in a mix solution of ethanol and acetic acid (4010 ml) and stirred for half an hour at room temperature (Solution A).Secondly, the required amount of urea, Cu(NO 3 ) 2 3H 2 O or Ag NO 3 were dissolved in 25 ml mix solution of ethanol and acetic acid (205 ml) under vigorous stirring at room temperature for 20 min (solution B).Then in the next step, solution B was added drop wise to solution A with rate 2 ml per minute under vigorous stirring within 12.5 min.For homogeneous solution, the mixture was stirrer for another 3 h at 50 C.After this, obtained mixture was kept at room temperature in air for 24 h to form homogeneous gel.The prepared TiO 2 based gels were dried at 80 C in microwave oven for 10 h.The finally obtained powders were calcined at 450 C for 3 h in the muffle furnace.The molar ratio of TiO 2 to urea was controlled as 1:2 in all nitrogen doped and co-doped samples, while Cu and Ag ratio to TiO 2 were used as1 atm. and 0.09 wt respectively which were previously optimized in our reports 30,31. The crystalline structural properties were studied using X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Dmax-3B) with Cu K radiation (V40 kV, I36 mA).The transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-1200EX electron microscope) operated at 200 kV was used for particle size and shape.The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET, ASAP 2020) surface areas (SBET) of the samples were calculated by nitrogen physisorption on a nitrogen adsorption apparatus at 77 K (Micromeritics Instruments).For the determination of electronic states and composition of prepared samples, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo-VG Scientific, ESCALAB250, monochromatic Al K Xray source) was used and binding energies were referenced to the C 1s peak of adventitious carbon at 284.8 eV.The UVVis diffuse reflectance spectra were measured on HITACHI U-4100 UV-vis spectrometer using BaSO 4 as a reference standard.The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were obtained using a HITACHI F-4500 Fluorescence spectrophotometer at excitation wavelength of 380 nm at room temperature. The photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 was performed in a gas closed circulation system under visible light irradiation at controlled temperature of 288 K with pressure of around 1.25 atm.A 300 W xenon lamp (Perfect Light PLSSXE300) with an optical filter (420 nm) was used as a light source.Certain amount of catalyst (50 mg) dispersed in deionized water (100 ml) by ultrasonic treatment in the reaction system.After evacuating the photo reactor, compressed CO 2 was purged into the system from inlet tube in the cylindrical quartz reactor.The resultant gas samples were analyzed online using a gas chromatograph (Agilent 7890A).

light source.source: The photocatalytic conversion of CO 2 into methane was carried out under visible light irradiation (420 nm) by prepared photocatalysts in order to evaluate the photocatalytic efficiency.
light source.context: Photocatalytic reduction of carbon dioxide (CO 2 ) into valuable hydrocarbon such as methane (CH 4 ) using water as reducing agent is a good strategy for environment and energy applications.In this study, a facile and simple sol-gel method was employed for the synthesis of metal (Cu and Ag) loaded nanosized NTiO 2 photocatalyst.The prepared photocatalysts were characterized by X-ray diffraction, transmission electron microscopy, BET Surface area analyzer, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and UV-vis diffuses reflectance spectroscopy.The photocatalytic conversion of CO 2 into methane was carried out under visible light irradiation (420 nm) by prepared photocatalysts in order to evaluate the photocatalytic efficiency.The results demonstrate that Ag loaded NTiO 2 showed enhanced photocatalytic performance for methane production from CO 2 compared to other Cu-NTiO 2 , NTiO 2 and TiO 2 photocatalysts.The improvement in the photocatalytic activity could be attributed to high specific surface area, extended visible light absorption and suppressed recombination of electronhole pair due to synergistic effects of silver and nitrogen in the Ag-NTiO 2 photocatalyst.This study demonstrates that Ag-NTiO 2 is a promising photocatalytic material for photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 into hydrocarbons under visible light irradiation.

lamp.source: A 300 W xenon lamp (Perfect Light PLSSXE300) with an optical filter (420 nm) was used as a light source.
lamp.context: In the typical process, the nitrogen doped TiO 2 and metal ions (MCu and Ag) and nitrogen co-doped TiO 2 were prepared using solgel method according to our previous study with little modification 29.Firstly, required amount of tetra-butyl titanate was slowly dissolved in a mix solution of ethanol and acetic acid (4010 ml) and stirred for half an hour at room temperature (Solution A).Secondly, the required amount of urea, Cu(NO 3 ) 2 3H 2 O or Ag NO 3 were dissolved in 25 ml mix solution of ethanol and acetic acid (205 ml) under vigorous stirring at room temperature for 20 min (solution B).Then in the next step, solution B was added drop wise to solution A with rate 2 ml per minute under vigorous stirring within 12.5 min.For homogeneous solution, the mixture was stirrer for another 3 h at 50 C.After this, obtained mixture was kept at room temperature in air for 24 h to form homogeneous gel.The prepared TiO 2 based gels were dried at 80 C in microwave oven for 10 h.The finally obtained powders were calcined at 450 C for 3 h in the muffle furnace.The molar ratio of TiO 2 to urea was controlled as 1:2 in all nitrogen doped and co-doped samples, while Cu and Ag ratio to TiO 2 were used as1 atm. and 0.09 wt respectively which were previously optimized in our reports 30,31. The crystalline structural properties were studied using X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Dmax-3B) with Cu K radiation (V40 kV, I36 mA).The transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-1200EX electron microscope) operated at 200 kV was used for particle size and shape.The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET, ASAP 2020) surface areas (SBET) of the samples were calculated by nitrogen physisorption on a nitrogen adsorption apparatus at 77 K (Micromeritics Instruments).For the determination of electronic states and composition of prepared samples, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo-VG Scientific, ESCALAB250, monochromatic Al K Xray source) was used and binding energies were referenced to the C 1s peak of adventitious carbon at 284.8 eV.The UVVis diffuse reflectance spectra were measured on HITACHI U-4100 UV-vis spectrometer using BaSO 4 as a reference standard.The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were obtained using a HITACHI F-4500 Fluorescence spectrophotometer at excitation wavelength of 380 nm at room temperature. The photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 was performed in a gas closed circulation system under visible light irradiation at controlled temperature of 288 K with pressure of around 1.25 atm.A 300 W xenon lamp (Perfect Light PLSSXE300) with an optical filter (420 nm) was used as a light source.Certain amount of catalyst (50 mg) dispersed in deionized water (100 ml) by ultrasonic treatment in the reaction system.After evacuating the photo reactor, compressed CO 2 was purged into the system from inlet tube in the cylindrical quartz reactor.The resultant gas samples were analyzed online using a gas chromatograph (Agilent 7890A).

reaction medium.source: Photocatalytic reduction of carbon dioxide (CO 2 ) into valuable hydrocarbon such as methane (CH 4 ) using water as reducing agent is a good strategy for environment and energy applications.
reaction medium.context: Photocatalytic reduction of carbon dioxide (CO 2 ) into valuable hydrocarbon such as methane (CH 4 ) using water as reducing agent is a good strategy for environment and energy applications.In this study, a facile and simple sol-gel method was employed for the synthesis of metal (Cu and Ag) loaded nanosized NTiO 2 photocatalyst.The prepared photocatalysts were characterized by X-ray diffraction, transmission electron microscopy, BET Surface area analyzer, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and UV-vis diffuses reflectance spectroscopy.The photocatalytic conversion of CO 2 into methane was carried out under visible light irradiation (420 nm) by prepared photocatalysts in order to evaluate the photocatalytic efficiency.The results demonstrate that Ag loaded NTiO 2 showed enhanced photocatalytic performance for methane production from CO 2 compared to other Cu-NTiO 2 , NTiO 2 and TiO 2 photocatalysts.The improvement in the photocatalytic activity could be attributed to high specific surface area, extended visible light absorption and suppressed recombination of electronhole pair due to synergistic effects of silver and nitrogen in the Ag-NTiO 2 photocatalyst.This study demonstrates that Ag-NTiO 2 is a promising photocatalytic material for photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 into hydrocarbons under visible light irradiation.

reaction medium.source: Certain amount of catalyst (50 mg) dispersed in deionized water (100 ml) by ultrasonic treatment in the reaction system.
reaction medium.context: In the typical process, the nitrogen doped TiO 2 and metal ions (MCu and Ag) and nitrogen co-doped TiO 2 were prepared using solgel method according to our previous study with little modification 29.Firstly, required amount of tetra-butyl titanate was slowly dissolved in a mix solution of ethanol and acetic acid (4010 ml) and stirred for half an hour at room temperature (Solution A).Secondly, the required amount of urea, Cu(NO 3 ) 2 3H 2 O or Ag NO 3 were dissolved in 25 ml mix solution of ethanol and acetic acid (205 ml) under vigorous stirring at room temperature for 20 min (solution B).Then in the next step, solution B was added drop wise to solution A with rate 2 ml per minute under vigorous stirring within 12.5 min.For homogeneous solution, the mixture was stirrer for another 3 h at 50 C.After this, obtained mixture was kept at room temperature in air for 24 h to form homogeneous gel.The prepared TiO 2 based gels were dried at 80 C in microwave oven for 10 h.The finally obtained powders were calcined at 450 C for 3 h in the muffle furnace.The molar ratio of TiO 2 to urea was controlled as 1:2 in all nitrogen doped and co-doped samples, while Cu and Ag ratio to TiO 2 were used as1 atm. and 0.09 wt respectively which were previously optimized in our reports 30,31. The photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 was performed in a gas closed circulation system under visible light irradiation at controlled temperature of 288 K with pressure of around 1.25 atm.A 300 W xenon lamp (Perfect Light PLSSXE300) with an optical filter (420 nm) was used as a light source.Certain amount of catalyst (50 mg) dispersed in deionized water (100 ml) by ultrasonic treatment in the reaction system.After evacuating the photo reactor, compressed CO 2 was purged into the system from inlet tube in the cylindrical quartz reactor.The resultant gas samples were analyzed online using a gas chromatograph (Agilent 7890A).
